dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, responding for a Sick Person call at First Aid only.
Hi, I'm Nick with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
My sister, she has a history of mental illness and occasional heroin use. She's flipping out and won't stop flailing.
I'm Alex, your EMT partner. Can you help us by telling us what's going on?
What seems to be the main problem now?
She's not coherent and won't stop moving. I can barely keep her from falling or hurting herself.
Is she having trouble breathing or in any pain?
She's not in distress, but is visibly impaired and not responding clearly to commands or basic direction.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name, please?
laughing* Sarah Button! I am unbreakable!
She is awake but is not following commands or making logical responses.
Is she keeping her eyes open on her own?
Yes, she has her eyes open, although she's not orientated to environment.
Can you tell me where you are right now, ma'am?
laughs* I'm in [CITY], I think. Or maybe another planet! You can't stop me!
Does she know she's sick or that the ambulance is here?
She keeps repeating that she's okay, but clearly something's off-and she also mixed tears with laughter.
Partner, does she follow any commands or respond to touch in a calm way?
No, her movements are chaotic and she shows no controlled response to external touch or direction.
Eyes, verbal, motor, orientation-GCS is 10 with E2 V4 M4, consistent with severely altered mental status.
Considering her airway status, no signs of vocal cord dysfunction or obstruction reported on scene.
She hasn't shown signs of airway compromise, despite flaying.
Ma'am, do you understand what we're saying and doing for you today?
She's loopy and needs restraints for safety, especially with how she's moving.
Planning physical restraint as part of initial stabilizing steps per protocol.
Any sign of shortness of breath or accessory muscle use?
No, her breathing is fast but normal-no distress visible at present.
Respirations: 14 breaths per minute, unlabored, without distress seen in her breath pattern.
No signs of wheezing, stridor, or audible adventitious sounds noted in initial assessment.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on ambient air, per oximeter at 18:27.
Pulse is 98 beats per minute, regular to the touch.
Glucose is 94 mg/dL-a normal initial reading.
Normal readings observed at the time.
Peripheral perfusion appears adequate. Skin is warm.
Hands are warm and pink, as far as I can tell with her erratic movements.
Blood pressure will be checked momentarily.
Skin appears intact and dry with no visible cuts, bruises, rashes, or recent trauma.
Pupils are 2 mm bilaterally, non-reactive with a penlight test.
Trachea is midline. No jugular venous distention. Head and face appear atraumatic today.
Any unusual discharge from her nose or mouth that may point to a source of instability?
No, just the emotional sounds as she keeps laughing and crying in fits.
Chest movement is unremarkable, breath sounds clear bilaterally.
No tenderness, guarding, or visible rigidity on inspection or physical contact.
Abdomen is soft and non-distended during exam. No GI concerns noted.
Pelvis is stable with no signs of instability or external genitourinary trauma noted.
Back is atraumatic and no signs of injury. Extremities are intact.
Motor skills noted as intact consistent with PMSx4.
Ma'am, do you know of any allergies you might have?
laughs* I think everyone is allergic to something.
Is she taking any prescribed or over-the-counter medications at this time?
She has a non-prescription use of heroin-usually snorted, but not clear if today it was mixed with anything else.
Any preexisting medical conditions we should be aware of related to this incident?
She has a documented history of schizoaffective disorder in the EPCR.
When did she last eat or drink before the event?
She had something at dinner, perhaps within two hours ago-but she doesn't track meals closely.
Do you feel pain or discomfort right now?
Not at all!
If you do feel any pain, can you rate it for us?
Ma'am, we are about to give you a medication to help calm her down.
Administering 5 mg of Versed IM per protocol, due to physical agitation identified today.
Applying physical restraints to stabilize her for safe transport as needed in this case.
IV placed in right hand for medication access and monitoring, in conjunction with restraints.
Heart rate: now 104 beats per minute.
Breathing rate: 12 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 101 over 62 mmHg.
SpO2 is at 100% with 3 Lpm nasal cannula in place.
Glucose is detected as 0 mg/dL during second readings.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia and constant readings observed during this episode.
Patient is showing signs of mild hypercapnea-suggesting beginning CO2 retention.
Applying four-lead ECG monitoring as field protocol for cardiac evaluation.
Heart rate now 96 beats per minute.
Respirations remain at 12 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is 102 over 58 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation at 98 on room air.
Glucose remains 0 as per recent repeat measurement.
EKG is now showing sinus rhythm.
Following protocol 3-4 for chemical and physical restraints due to inadequate motor control.
All findings and actions relayed to Hayley Trinidad, RN, in verbal EPCR at 18:39.
Vital signs at onset were 98 bpm, 14 rpm, 110/58 mmHg, 99% spo2, 94 glucose.
Reapplication of restraints required due to movement during transport observed in transport.
Pain still reported at 0. Oxygen at 3 Lpm.
Transport destination set to [COMPANY_NAME] for behavioral emergency care.
Patient moved to ER using 5-point restraints and draw sheet per hospital procedure.
She remained with minimal reactivity during the entire transport as per EPCR.
Behavioral management per protocol confirmed in action without incident.
Restrains removed upon arrival and transfer complete to hospital staff.
Handoff fully coordinated with nursing staff in charge.
Report given to Hayley Trinidad, RN, with full EPCR documentation.
Patient now under hospital supervision. EMS duties complete per EPCR protocol.
Blood pressure initially recorded as 110/58 per first assessment.
Skin is warm, dry and intact with no signs of petechia, purpura, or other dermatologic findings.
